question_title: How many cumulative norovirus outbreaks will CDC NoroSTAT report for the 2025–2026 season as of June 12, 2026?
summary: The forecast is dominated by uncertainty about whether CDC will publish a NoroSTAT data update before the June 12 resolution date. The CDC page explicitly states data is "typically released online on a monthly basis." The last update was around May 20 (showing data through May 7, with 1,194 cumulative outbreaks). The next monthly update is expected around June 17-20, likely AFTER the resolution date. I estimate ~45% probability that no update occurs by June 12, leaving the value at exactly 1,194. If an update does occur, 4-5 weeks of additional data at this season's below-median trajectory would add approximately 40-65 outbreaks (median ~48). This creates a highly bimodal distribution with a large point mass at 1194 and a secondary peak around 1235-1245. The 2025-2026 season is tracking within the historical IQR but below the median, with sharp late-spring decline (19 outbreaks in the week of May 1, declining to ~7-12/week by late May).

factors.description: Data update cadence is monthly, not weekly. CDC data table page says 'typically released online on a monthly basis.' Last update was May 20; next expected June 17-20, likely AFTER the June 12 resolution date. ~45% probability page still shows 1,194.
If an update DOES occur by June 12, 4-5 weeks of new data would add ~40-65 outbreaks. This season tracks at/below the historical median (30th percentile based on recent weeks).
Season is in sharp late-spring decline. Week of May 1 had 19 outbreaks; by late May historical IQR drops to 7-12/week. Very modest incremental activity even if data updates.
Question author expects '~5 additional weekly updates' — may have inside knowledge about update schedule, or may be mistaken about cadence. Mild upward pressure from the possibility they are right.
Hard floor at 1194 (cumulative count can only increase). This creates strong left-truncation and a large probability mass at the floor value.

reasoning:
**Summary of my forecast:**

This question has a highly **bimodal structure** driven by whether CDC updates the NoroSTAT data before June 12, 2026.

**Key finding:** The CDC data table page explicitly states data is *"typically released online on a monthly basis."* The last update was around May 20 (showing data through May 7). The next monthly update is expected around June 17-20 — **after** the resolution date.

**Distribution characteristics:**
- **~45% probability** the page still shows **1,194** on June 12 (no update)
- **~55% probability** of an update, in which case the value would likely be **1,220-1,260**
- **Median: ~1,218** | **P10: 1,194** | **P90: 1,255**
- Hard floor at 1,194 (cumulative count can't decrease)
- Essentially zero probability of exceeding 1,280

The dominant uncertainty is **data publication timing** (CDC operational behavior), not outbreak activity (which follows well-established seasonal decline patterns).
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many cumulative norovirus outbreaks will CDC NoroSTAT report for the 2025–2026 season as of June 12, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43751)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as the cumulative number of norovirus outbreaks reported for the 2025–2026 season by NoroSTAT-participating states, as published on the CDC NoroSTAT Data page (https://www.cdc.gov/norovirus/php/reporting/norostat-data.html) when checked at 23:59 UTC on June 12, 2026. The relevant figure is the count described on that page as outbreaks reported during "August 1, 2025 – [latest date]" for NoroSTAT-participating states. For example, a published value of "1,350 outbreaks" resolves as 1350. If the CDC page is temporarily unavailable on June 12, 2026, the most recent archived snapshot from the Wayback Machine (https://web.archive.org/web/*/https://www.cdc.gov/norovirus/php/reporting/norostat-data.html) captured on or before June 12, 2026 will be used.
fine_print: If CDC restructures the page such that a 2025–2026 cumulative seasonal total starting August 1, 2025 is no longer published, but a more detailed NoroSTAT data table (linked from the same page) provides weekly outbreak counts for NoroSTAT states, the resolver may sum weekly counts from the week beginning August 1, 2025 through the most recent reported week as of June 12, 2026. If neither the summary page nor the linked detailed table provides usable 2025–2026 data by June 12, 2026, the question resolves based on the most recently available cumulative figure published since May 7, 2026.

factors.description: Data update cadence is monthly, not weekly. CDC page says 'typically released on a monthly basis.' Last update was May 20, next expected June 17-20, likely AFTER June 12 resolution date.
If an update DOES occur by June 12, 4-5 weeks of new data would add ~40-65 outbreaks at the 30th percentile. This season has been tracking at/below the historical median.
Season is in sharp late-spring decline. Week of May 1 had 19 outbreaks; by late May this drops to 7-12/week historically. Very little incremental activity even if data updates.
Question author expects '~5 additional weekly updates' - suggests they may know something about update cadence that I don't, or may be mistaken.
Hard floor at 1194 (cumulative count can only increase). This creates strong left-truncation.

input.anchor: Current cumulative count of 1,194 outbreaks as of May 7, 2026. Historical late-season additions (May-July) are typically 100-170 total, but the question of how much additional data will be published by June 12 dominates the forecast.
input.assessment: This question has a highly bimodal structure driven by whether CDC updates the NoroSTAT data before June 12. The CDC page explicitly states data is released "on a monthly basis." The last update was May 20 (showing data through May 7). The next monthly update would logically be around June 17-20, which falls AFTER the resolution date. I estimate ~45% probability of no update, which would leave the value at exactly 1,194. If an update does occur, 4-5 additional weeks of data at this season's below-median trajectory would add ~40-65 outbreaks (median ~45). The bimodal nature creates a median around 1218 with a very long left tail at 1194.
input.calibration_notes: The main uncertainty is NOT about outbreak activity (which follows well-established seasonal patterns) but about DATA PUBLICATION timing. This makes the question more about CDC operational behavior than epidemiology. The distribution is bimodal: ~45% at 1194 and ~55% in the 1220-1260 range.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether CDC publishes a data update between now (June 2) and June 12 - this is the dominant uncertainty. 2. If they do update, how many weeks of new data will be included. 3. Whether the question author has inside knowledge about the update schedule.
input.tool_audit: research() returned excellent data: full weekly historical distributions, current season data, genotype information. fetch_url on the CDC page confirmed data is still through May 7 as of June 2. The critical finding was from the data table page which explicitly states 'Data from the NoroSTAT network are typically released online on a monthly basis.' Monte Carlo simulation worked well for modeling the bimodal structure.
input.update_triggers: CDC publishing a data update to the NoroSTAT page before June 12 would immediately shift the distribution to the 1220-1270 range. Conversely, if June 12 arrives with no update, the value is almost certainly 1194.
input.process_reflection: This question turned out to be primarily about CDC data publication timing rather than epidemiology. The research tools effectively gathered the historical weekly data, but the key insight came from fetching the actual CDC pages and discovering the monthly update cadence. The simulation framework handled the bimodal structure well. One process gap: I couldn't easily access Wayback Machine to determine the exact historical update pattern of the CDC page, which would have been very informative for estimating P(update by June 12).

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Hallucinated evidence — None found
All factor claims are grounded in the research trace:
- **Factor 1** ("monthly cadence, last update May 20, next expected June 17-20"): Confirmed by fetch_url of the data table page returning "Data from the NoroSTAT network are typically released online on a monthly basis" and the research entry stating "Data is updated monthly; the latest update was May 20, 2026." The direct fetch of the CDC summary page confirmed data still shows 1,194 through May 7 as of June 2.
- **Factor 2** ("4-5 weeks add ~40-65 outbreaks at 30th percentile"): Confirmed by the simulation output showing "Total for 5 weeks at 30th percentile: ~54" and the detailed weekly IQR data from the CDC data table.
- **Factor 3** ("sharp late-spring decline, May 1 had 19 outbreaks, late May 7-12/week"): Confirmed by research data showing week of May 1 = 19 outbreaks and historical p25=7/p75=12 for week of May 29.
- **Factor 4** ("Question author expects ~5 weekly updates"): Confirmed from Metaculus question background: "roughly five additional weekly updates will have been incorporated."
- **Factor 5** ("Hard floor at 1194"): Confirmed from question metadata lower_bound=1194.

### Double-counting — None found
Factors 1 and 2 address different aspects (update cadence vs. conditional outbreak volume). Factors 3 and 2 share some overlap in that both describe declining late-season activity, but Factor 3 adds the specific current-season data point (19 outbreaks for May 1) that justifies positioning the season within the historical range. The logit magnitudes are modest enough that any overlap doesn't distort the result.

### Wrong-direction factors — None found
All logit signs align with factor descriptions. Factor 1 pushes toward lower values (data staying at 1194), Factor 2 pushes up conditional on an update, Factor 3 pushes down (sharp decline = less to add), Factor 4 pushes up (someone may know updates are coming), Factor 5 is neutral (floor constraint).

### Contradictory assessment — None found
The assessment describes a bimodal distribution (45% at 1194, remainder around 1230-1260), yielding a median of ~1218. This is consistent with the simulation results and the factor logic.

### Missing resolution criteria — Not an issue
Resolution criteria are fully provided and engaged with. The agent correctly identifies that the question resolves based on the cumulative number shown on the CDC page on June 12, and correctly explores what the page actually shows today.

### Resolution misalignment — None found
The agent directly fetched the CDC resolution page (norostat-data.html) and confirmed the current state. The analysis correctly focuses on what number will be displayed on June 12.

### Counterargument quality — Adequate
The counterargument substantively challenges the 45% no-update probability, noting the question author's apparent expertise, the possibility of separate update schedules for the data table vs. summary page, and the risk of anchoring on a single observation. This constructs a coherent alternative story.

### Confidence assessment — Appropriate
0.35 confidence is well-calibrated given the dominant binary uncertainty (will CDC update before June 12?) that the agent cannot resolve from available evidence.

### Probability assessment — Reasonable
The submitted center of 1218 with range 1194-1255 is consistent with the final Monte Carlo simulation (P(no update)=45%, median=1218, P10=1194, P90=1255). The bimodal structure is well-modeled. I would estimate similarly: the evidence strongly supports ~40-50% probability the page won't update by June 12, creating a large mass at 1194, with the conditional-on-update median around 1235-1245. A blended median of ~1218 is reasonable. I don't see a meaningful gap between my estimate and the agent's.

### Tool audit — Consistent
The agent claims good research results and successful fetch_url calls. Metrics confirm 15 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate), consistent with the agent's narrative.
